front.title: Simplifying BUM forwarding in LISP based VxLAN fabrics

abstract.t: In a Locator/ID Separation Protocol (LISP) network where EID Layer 2 mobility is enabled between RLOCs as per defined in , the forwarding of overlay Broadcast, Unknown unicast, and link-local Multicast (BUM) traffic is achieved either by sending an encapsulated unicast copy of the packets to all interested RLOCs or by sending an encapsulated multicast packet to the provider network and letting it do the replication as close as possible to the destination RLOCs.

When using multicast replication, relies on Protocol Independent Multicast - Any Source Multicast (PIM ASM) and the use of Rendezvous Points (RPs) within the provider network to discover RLOC participation to a L2VNI.

The role of the underlay is to provide a robust and stable transport network that is configured once and rarely touched afterwards. Importanty, it should be independent from any overlay networks that are dynamic by nature. Although as explained above, there is currently a direct dependency between one or several underlaying PIM RP(s) and Layer 2 overlay networks supporting BUM. This defeats the principle of isolation.

This document describes an different approach for BUM traffic handling within LISP networks by utilising PIM Source-Specific Multicast (SSM) instead of PIM ASM in the provider networks and integrating L2VNI RLOC membership discovery directly into the LISP control plane. The outlined approach eliminates the need for RPs in provider networks and allow the overlay protocol LISP to shape the required underlay PIM SSM multicast trees.

This method ensure dynamic native multicast tree formation based on overlay need, reduces the configuration and troubleshooting burden on network administrators, ensuring more resilient and streamlined BUM traffic forwarding within layer 2 LISP fabrics.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: The Locator/ID Separation Protocol (LISP) has been instrumental in advancing the capabilities of network fabrics by separating endpoint identifiers and their location within the network infrastructure. However, the facilitation of Broadcast, Unknown unicast, and Multicast (BUM) traffic within a LISP-enabled environment, especially when layer 2 mobility is a consideration, presents notable challenges. Among these is the efficient forwarding of BUM traffic to the relevant Router Locators (RLOCs) without inducing unicast replication in the underlay network.

In the current framework, the forwarding of encapsulated BUM traffic within a LISP-based fabric relies heavily on the use of Protocol Independent Multicast - Any Source Multicast (PIM ASM) and associated Rendezvous Points (RPs) within the provider's network. This dependency creates a single point of failure and introduces operational complexities due to the potential need for multiple RPs and the associated maintenance burden.

This RFC proposes an alternative method aimed at simplifying BUM traffic forwarding by leveraging PIM Source-Specific Multicast (SSM) within LISP-based networks that utilize layer 2 Virtual Network Identifiers (L2VNI). The approach outlined herein delegates the responsibility of multicast source discovery from the provider network's underlay to the LISP control plane itself. By integrating dynamic (S,G) multicast tree creation triggered by LISP control-plane messaging, this solution seeks to reduce the complexity of the provider network's role to ensuring basic unicast reachability between RLOCs and supporting PIM SSM for multicast routing.

The scope of this RFC is focused exclusively on LISP networks with layer 2 extensions, addressing the specific case of BUM traffic forwarding. The document will detail the mechanisms by which the LISP control plane can manage multicast source discovery and facilitate the establishment of efficient multicast forwarding paths without the need for traditional PIM ASM constructs and Rendezvous Points.

The intended audience for this RFC includes network architects, engineers, and administrators tasked with managing LISP-based fabrics, particularly those who seek to optimize their networks for layer 2 mobility.

section.name: Requirements Language
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

section.name: Definition of Terms
section.t: LISP related terms, notably Map-Request, Map-Reply, Ingress Tunnel Router (ITR), Egress Tunnel Router (ETR), Map-Server (MS) and Map-Resolver (MR) are defined in the LISP specifications and .
Terms defining interactions with the LISP Mapping System are defined in .
Terms related to the procedures for signal free multicast are defined in .
The following terms are here defined to facilitate the descriptions and discussions within this particular document.
Site-Overlay - Overlay network at a specific area or domain. This overlay network has a dedicated Mapping System.
Border-xTR - xTR that connects a site-overlay to one or more uberlays.
xTR - LISP Tunnel Router as defined in . An xTR connects end-points to the site-overlay.
Local Mapping System - Mapping system of the site-overlay

Uberlay - Overlay network that interconnects different site-overlays to each other. The Uberlay has a dedicated Mapping System and creates an overlay amongst the border xTRs which connect different site-overlays.
Uberlay Mapping System - Autonomous mapping system dedicated to the uberlay.

Site-Overlay EIDs - Also referred to as local site-overlay EIDs, these are the EIDs that are connected to xTRs in a particular site-overlay and are registered in their own local site-overlay mapping system. These EIDs will also be registered in the uberlay but not in the remote site-overlay mapping systems.
Remote site-overlay EIDs - These are EIDs connected and registered in site-overlays other than the local site-overlay.
Local site-overlay EIDs - These are EIDs connected and registered in the local site-overlay.

section.name: Prerequisites
section.t: Every network devices participating in a common L2VNI MUST use a common PIM SSM group in the RLOC space to transport BUM traffic.

section.name: First network device to join the L2VNI
section.t: When the first network device joins an L2VNI where BUM is allowed, it MUST register it’s intent to send and receive multicast traffic in the RLOC space for the group that has been assigned to the L2VNI. This is done by sending a LISP Map-Register to the LISP Map Server containing the Layer2 IID, it’s RLOC address and the PIM SSM Group on which the BUM traffic will be mapped. The Map-Server stores a new record in the database and triggers a lookup to select all entries of the table. A Map-Notify containing (L2IID, Vector[list of all RLOCs present in the L2VNI], Multicast Group) MUST be sent to all results of this lookup. In this specific scenario, the first joiner will only see it’s own RLOC address in the vector list and that will serve as a confirmation that it has join the L2VNI.

section.name: Additional network devices joining the L2VNI
section.t: Following the same process described in 3.1, the network device sends a Map-Register to the MS containing the L2IID to join, it’s RLOC address and the PIM SSM Group for BUM transport. At this point, the Map Server looks up the database for the provided L2IID and founds an existing entry. It adds the new RLOC in the vector list of RLOCs participating to the L2VNI and verifies that the PIM SSM group is matching. It then sends a Map-Notify (L2IID, Vector[All RLOCs in the L2VNI], Multicast Group) to all members of the Vector including the new joiner.

The new joiner receives a confirmation that it has successfully joined the L2VNI by seeing its RLOC in the Vector. It also discover the list of all other members of the L2VNI and do a lookup in the MRIB to see if a multicast tree already exists to them. Since it is new to the L2VNI, no entries should be present and a PIM SSM Join will be sent towards all the members to receive the encapsulated BUM traffic from them.

All others members of the L2VNI receiving the Map-Notify will do the same by verifying their own membership. They will also lookup for existing multicast tree entries in their MRIB for all other RLOCs present in the Vector list. Since the new joiner is not present, they will all send a PIM SSM Join to it.
At this point, a full-mesh of multicast trees should be created between all members of the L2VNI. This allows BUM traffic transport across a PIM SSM core network.

section.name: Graceful leave operations
section.t: When removing a network device gracefully from an L2VNI, it MUST send a PIM Leave to all members of the L2VNI. After doing this, a MAP-Register MUST be sent to the MAP SERVER containing (L2IID, it’s RLOC, Multicast Group) plus a TTL=0 to signal that this record has expired. The MAP SERVER deletes the requestor RLOC from Vector list and notifies every one of this change by sending a MAP-NOTIFY with a new vector list. All the other Network Devices compare the vector list to the MRIB and find out that there is an extra entry in the MRIB compared to the Vector list and send a PIM Leave to that RLOC.

section.name: Bidirectional multicast trees
section.t: Depending on the network size, a full mesh of multicast trees (between all the RLOC participating to an L2VNI) can create a huge number of entries in the MRIB. To reduce this amount, it might be beneficial to manipulate the topology to force the multicast traffic to pass through a central point.

section.name: A root node is joining an L2VNI
section.t: Initially, the Map-Server is rejecting any unflagged MAP-Register from network devices willing to join a L2VNI configured with a hub-n-spoke topology. A network device that has been configured as root (TBD) sends a MAP-Register to the Map-Server with a new entry (L2IID, it’s RLOC, Multicast Group) and a special Flag (TBD) that identifies itself as root for the tree. The MS accept the registration and add a new entry to the DB as (L2IID, Vector[RLOC] (with flag set if root), Multicast group). The MS sends a MAP-NOTIFY to all devices in the Vector (only one is currently in the vector). The Root node can validate that it has successfully joined the L2VNI. The MS is now ready to accept registrations from other network devices.

section.name: Non-root nodes are joining the L2VNI
section.t: When additional non-root nodes join the L2VNI by sending a (L2IID, their RLOCs, Multicast Group) Map-Register, the MapServer is storing new entries in the Vector of devices participating in the L2VNI. The MapServer then sends MAP-Notify to the nodes with a vector of RLOCs containing the Root node and the respective RLOCs of the joining devices. That means that means that at anytime, a new device joining the L2VNI will receive a list of root nodes plus its own RLOC confirming that it has successfully joined the L2VNI. The Root nodes are also notified that new members are joining the L2VNI through a Map-Notify containing a vector of all non-root RLOCs.

section.name: Multicast Tree formation
section.t: The Root Tree identities the new nodes and issue a PIM SSM Join towards them. The non-root nodes will initiate PIM SSM join the root nodes only. This results is a bi-directionnal multicast tree passing through the root node.

section.name: Root node redundancy
section.t: Since the data traffic is passing through the root nodes it is critical to be able to provide redundancy to those nodes. If more than one root node has been registered in the L2VNI, the locator priority will be used to determined which one will receive the PIM Joins from the non-root nodes. The secondary root node will track the reachability of the primary root node in the RIB. When the primary node fails, the IGP will remove the RLOC for the RIB and make the LISP next-hop unreachable. This will trigger a new PIM Join to the secondary root node. At this point, the secondary root node will also detect that the primary root node is unavailable and will elect itself as the new primary root node. As candidate root node, it has also received a Vector of non-root nodes participating to the L2VNI and will trigger PIM SSM Joins towards them. The bidirectional multicast tree is rebuilt and BUM forwarding is restored. To prevent any interruptions by misconfigured root-nodes or failed primary root node coming back online, no pre-empt should be done when a new root node with a higher priority is added in the network.

section.name: Traffic Steering
section.t: In some situations, BUM transport is only required for IPv4 ARP and DHCP services to a gateway located outside of the fabric network. Such services require unidirectional BUM transport (ARP request and DHCP Discover) and the return traffic is unicast (ARP reply and DHCP Offer/Request/Ack). If the PxTR connected to the external gateway it should also be able to be configured as unidirectional root node for the L2VNI. That means that it should be able to receive BUM traffic from other non-root nodes and forward it outside of the fabric.

section.name: A unidirectional root node is joining the L2VNI
section.t: Similarly to , until the first root node is discovered the Map-Server will reject any other MAP-REGISTER. When a root node is configured to act as a unidirectional root node, it sends a MAP-REGISTER to the MAP-SERVER (L2IID, RLOC, Multicast Group) with two Flags (Root, Unidirectional). At this point, the MAP-Server is ready to receive non-root node MAP-Registers.

section.name: Non-root nodes are joining the L2VNI
section.t: When additional non-root nodes joins the L2VNI by sending a MAP-REGISTER (L2IID, RLOC, Multicast Group), the MS creates new entries in the DB. The MS sends a MAP-Notify back to the non-root nodes with their respective RLOC in the vector (like if they were the only node in the topology). The MS also sends a MAP-NOTIFY to the unidirectional root node with the list of new nodes in the topology.

section.name: Multicast Tree formation
section.t: After being notified by the MS, the unidirectional root node will initiate a PIM SSM Join towards the new non-root nodes to receive encapsulated BUM traffic from them. The non-root nodes are not aware of other nodes in the topology and will not initiate any PIM SSM join. This result of this is a unidirectional multicast tree where the xTR can send encapsulated BUM traffic to the PxTR. Since the return traffic is unicast, there is no needs to maintain multicast state in the underlay on non-root nodes.

section.name: Redundancy
section.t: There are two parts that come into play when considering the redundancy of unidirectional root-nodes. The first part relates to the point which remains almost the same except that the non-root nodes will not care about the switchover to another root node. When the primary root node fails, the secondary will detect it and issue a PIM SSM Join towards all the non-root to recreate a new multicast tree.

section.name: Coexistence with bidirectional root nodes
section.t: Both types of root nodes (unidirectional and bidirectional) MUST NOT coexist in a given L2VNI.

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This memo includes no request to IANA.

section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: This document should not affect the security of the Internet.

section.name: Acknowledgements
section.t: The authors want to thank Marc Portoles-Comeras and Alberto Rodriguez-Natal for their insightful contribution to shaping the ideas in this document.
